title: 3 Grammar Mistakes That Are Tanking Your English Grade
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have good ideas. You understand the book. You answer the prompt. But your grade is lower than you expect. The culprit is often small grammar errors that irritate teachers and make your writing look less polished. Here are the three most common mistakes and how to fix them.

Mistake 1: The Run-On Sentence (Fused Sentence)

A run-on sentence happens when you join two complete sentences with no punctuation or conjunction.

Wrong: 'I went to the store I bought some milk.'
Right: 'I went to the store, and I bought some milk.'
Also right: 'I went to the store. I bought some milk.'

How to check: Read your sentence out loud. If you naturally pause between two ideas, you probably need a period or a comma + conjunction.

Common run-on patterns: joining sentences with 'however' or 'therefore' without proper punctuation. 'I studied all night however I still failed' should be 'I studied all night; however, I still failed.'

Mistake 2: The Comma Splice

A comma splice is a specific type of run-on where you join two complete sentences with only a comma.

Wrong: 'I love pizza, it's my favorite food.'
Right: 'I love pizza. It's my favorite food.'
Also right: 'I love pizza, and it's my favorite food.'
Also right: 'I love pizza; it's my favorite food.'

A comma alone is not strong enough to hold two complete sentences together. Use a period, a semicolon, or a comma + conjunction (and, but, so, for, nor, yet, or).

One exception: very short, closely related sentences can sometimes use a comma. 'I came, I saw, I conquered.' This is stylistic, but when in doubt, don't do it.

Mistake 3: The Apostrophe Catastrophe

Apostrophes show possession or contraction. They never make a word plural.

Possession: 'The student's book' (one student). 'The students' book' (multiple students).
Contractions: 'It's' means 'it is.' 'Who's' means 'who is.' 'They're' means 'they are.'
Plurals: 'The 1990s' (not 1990's). 'The Smiths' (not Smith's).

The most common error: its vs. it's. 'Its' is possessive (belonging to it). 'It's' is contraction (it is). Many smart people get this wrong. Remember: if you can replace it with 'it is,' use an apostrophe. Otherwise, don't.

Example: 'The dog wagged its tail.' (Would 'it is tail' make sense? No.) Correct. 'It's raining outside.' ('It is raining.' Yes.) Correct.

How to Catch These Mistakes in Your Own Writing

Read your essay out loud. Your ear will catch awkward phrasing and missing punctuation that your eyes skip over. Read it backward (last sentence to first). This forces you to focus on each sentence individually.

Have someone else read it. A fresh pair of eyes catches errors you've looked at too many times.

Use Grammarly or your word processor's spell check. These tools aren't perfect, but they catch the most obvious errors. Don't rely on them completely — learn the rules yourself.

Fix these three errors, and your writing will instantly look more polished. Teachers will notice. Your grade will improve.
